title: Soft amaretti
subtitle: Italian almond biscuits, chewy in the centre and lightly crunchy outside
excerpt: Soft amaretti, the chewy rounded Italian almond biscuit, made with ground almonds, sugar, egg whites, and almond extract. Rolled in icing sugar before baking. The outside cracks and the inside stays soft and chewy. Gluten-free and dairy-free. Makes twenty.
sourceNotes: Amaretti are documented in Italian domestic cooking from the seventeenth century. Bartolomeo Stefani, L'arte di ben cucinare (1662): almond-based biscuits made with egg whites and sugar are documented as a standard Italian confection. The soft or morbidi style, a chewy cracked biscuit made with ground almonds and egg whites, is distinguished from the hard croccante style in Italian domestic baking manuals from the nineteenth century and in Italian culinary references available through the Internet Archive.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in a single layer, then bag. Defrost at room temperature for 30 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keep in an airtight tin for up to 1 week. They soften further with time, which is desirable.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf. The biscuits are done when the outside is cracked and lightly coloured and the surface feels set. The inside remains chewy when cold

glossaryTerms.definition: The thin, cracked sugar shell that forms on the outside of a soft amaretto as it bakes. The biscuit is rolled in icing sugar before baking; as the dough spreads and rises in the oven heat, the sugar coating cracks slightly, forming a papery shell around the chewy almond interior. The cracking is how you know the biscuit has baked correctly — a smooth, uncracked surface means the biscuit has not spread enough and needs more time.

content.text: Soft amaretti are one of the shortest biscuit recipes in the category: five ingredients, no flour, no butter, no chilling. The egg whites bind the almond and sugar into a paste; the icing sugar coating cracks in the oven to produce the distinctive surface. They are done when they crack and colour, not when a skewer comes out clean.

Preheat the oven to 170°C fan. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Mix together the {{almond-flour}}, {{caster-sugar}}, {{egg-whites}}, and {{almond-extract}} until a sticky paste forms. Spread the {{icing-sugar}} on a plate. Roll walnut-sized balls of the paste in the icing sugar until well coated and place on the lined tray, spacing them 4 cm apart. Bake for 14–16 minutes. Watch for the
: the surface should be cracked and the underside lightly coloured. Cool on the tray for 10 minutes before moving — they firm as they cool.
